name: Zhao Gongming
description: Zhao Gongming, also known as Zhao Xuantan, is a prominent figure in Chinese folk religion and mythology, often revered as a wealth god and a powerful protector. Originally a human during the late Shang Dynasty, he was a hermit and a skilled warrior. He gained prominence through his role in the classic novel *Investiture of the Gods* (Fengshen Yanyi), where he initially fought against the Zhou Dynasty forces. During the battles, he displayed formidable magical abilities, wielding powerful artifacts and commanding supernatural forces. Ultimately, he was killed in battle by a curse. After his death, he was appointed as the Black Tiger Altar Marshal, tasked with managing plagues and disasters. Over time, his role evolved, and he became widely venerated as a wealth god, particularly by merchants and businesses seeking prosperity and protection from financial misfortune. He is often depicted riding a black tiger, holding a golden rod, and surrounded by treasures. His stern visage and powerful aura inspire both respect and a sense of security. While sometimes associated with martial prowess, his primary function in modern worship is as a bringer of wealth and good fortune, ensuring success in commercial endeavors and safeguarding against financial ruin. He is considered one of the Five Wealth Gods (Wu Cai Shen) and is frequently invoked during business openings, festivals, and other auspicious occasions. His influence extends across various regions of China and among Chinese communities worldwide, solidifying his position as a significant deity in the Chinese pantheon.
subtitle: Wealth God and Protector Deity

keyMyths.title: Investiture of the Gods
keyMyths.description: Zhao Gongming appears in Fengshen Yanyi as a powerful warrior fighting for the Shang Dynasty. He is eventually defeated and killed by Jiang Ziya's forces.

keyMyths.title: Appointment as Black Tiger Altar Marshal
keyMyths.description: After his death, Zhao Gongming is appointed as the Black Tiger Altar Marshal. This role involves managing plagues and disasters, showcasing his power.

keyMyths.title: Evolution into a Wealth God
keyMyths.description: Over time, Zhao Gongming's role evolved from a marshal to a widely venerated wealth god. He is now primarily associated with bringing wealth and prosperity.

sources.author: Xu Zhonglin
sources.description: A classic Ming dynasty novel that features Zhao Gongming as a key character.
sources.text: A classic Ming dynasty novel that features Zhao Gongming as a key character.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: Collection of folk tales and religious practices that detail the worship of Zhao Gongming.
sources.text: Collection of folk tales and religious practices that detail the worship of Zhao Gongming.

worshipPractices.name: Household Altars
worshipPractices.description: Many families maintain altars dedicated to Zhao Gongming in their homes, where they offer incense, food, and prayers for prosperity and protection. These altars are often adorned with images or statues of the deity.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Veneration
worshipPractices.description: Temples dedicated to Zhao Gongming are found throughout China and other Chinese communities worldwide. Devotees visit these temples to make offerings, seek blessings, and participate in religious ceremonies.

festivals.name: Zhao Gongming's Birthday
festivals.description: Celebrations are held on Zhao Gongming's birthday, which varies each year according to the lunar calendar. These celebrations often include temple fairs, theatrical performances, and special religious rituals.

festivals.name: Chinese New Year
festivals.description: Zhao Gongming is often invoked during Chinese New Year celebrations as a deity of wealth and prosperity. Images of Zhao Gongming may be displayed to attract good fortune for the coming year.

cultural.modernLegacy: Zhao Gongming remains a popular deity in modern Chinese society, particularly among business owners and entrepreneurs. His image is frequently used in advertisements and commercial settings to promote wealth and success. He is also featured in popular culture, including films, television shows, and video games, solidifying his status as a cultural icon.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The origins of Zhao Gongming are rooted in the late Shang Dynasty. Legend states that he was born as a human, leading a reclusive life as a hermit on Mount Zhongnan. He was known for his exceptional martial skills and mastery of magical arts. Historical accounts are scarce, and much of his early life is shrouded in folklore. However, it is believed that his reputation as a powerful warrior and recluse gradually transformed into that of a deity associated with wealth and protection. The *Investiture of the Gods* novel significantly shaped his identity, providing a narrative framework for his transformation from a mortal to a divine being. This narrative solidified his role in the Chinese pantheon and contributed to his widespread worship.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Zhao Gongming's most prominent story comes from the *Investiture of the Gods*. He initially sides with the Shang Dynasty against the Zhou, employing his magical powers to defeat numerous Zhou generals. He wields powerful artifacts and commands supernatural forces, proving a formidable opponent. However, he is eventually defeated and killed in battle. After his death, Jiang Ziya, the strategist of the Zhou Dynasty, deifies Zhao Gongming, appointing him as the "Black Tiger God" and placing him in charge of managing wealth and treasures. This pivotal moment transforms him from a warring figure into a deity of prosperity. Other minor myths depict him as a protector against evil spirits and a regulator of trade, further solidifying his multifaceted role in Chinese folklore.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: The worship of Zhao Gongming is widespread throughout China and Chinese communities worldwide. He is primarily venerated as a wealth god, believed to bring prosperity and good fortune to his devotees. Many businesses display images or statues of Zhao Gongming in their establishments, hoping to attract wealth and success. Household altars are also commonly dedicated to him, where families offer incense, food, and prayers. Temples dedicated to Zhao Gongming serve as centers for religious ceremonies and festivals, attracting devotees seeking blessings and guidance. The specific rituals and practices associated with his worship vary regionally, but the core belief in his ability to bestow wealth and protection remains consistent. His image is often depicted riding a black tiger, symbolizing his power and authority.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Zhao Gongming's legacy extends beyond his role as a wealth god. He embodies the values of integrity, bravery, and the potential for transformation. His story, popularized by *Investiture of the Gods*, has influenced Chinese popular culture for centuries. He appears in various forms of media, including operas, films, and video games, ensuring his continued relevance in modern society. His image serves as a symbol of prosperity and protection, frequently used in commercial advertising and art. Moreover, the evolution of his character, from a mortal warrior to a revered deity, reflects the dynamic nature of Chinese mythology and its ability to adapt to changing cultural values. He remains a potent symbol of aspiration and the pursuit of a better life.

shortDescription: Chinese wealth god and powerful protector, originally a hermit during the Shang Dynasty. Prominent in folk religion and *Investiture of the Gods*.
